subject: EU Law
lesson_title: The CILFIT Doctrine: When Courts of Last Instance May Not Refer

questions.question: Under Article 267(3) TFEU, courts of last instance have an obligation to refer questions of EU law to the CJEU. The CILFIT case [1982] established exceptions to this obligation. Which case is CILFIT?

options.A: CILFIT Srl and Lanificio di Gavardo SpA v Ministry of Health (Case 283/81)
options.C: Consorzio Italiano Lantifici Filati (CILFIT) v Council (Case 14/83)
options.D: CILFIT SpA v Italian Ministry of Finance (Case 271/81)

questions.explanation: CILFIT Srl and Lanificio di Gavardo SpA v Ministry of Health (Case 283/81, 1982) is the foundational case on the exceptions to the Article 267(3) obligation to refer. The case arose from Italian wool importers challenging a health inspection levy on wool. The Italian Court of Cassation referred the question of when a court of last instance need not refer a question to the CJEU, and the CJEU laid down the CILFIT doctrine.

questions.question: The first CILFIT exception — 'acte éclairé' — allows a court of last instance to refrain from referring where:

options.A: The question of EU law is obvious and needs no interpretation
options.B: The question raised is materially identical to one already answered by the CJEU in a previous preliminary ruling — the point has already been decided
options.C: The national court has consulted with other national supreme courts and they all agree on the answer
options.D: The Commission has issued a notice confirming the interpretation of the EU law in question

questions.explanation: Acte éclairé: where the question of interpretation raised is materially identical to a question that has already been the subject of a preliminary ruling by the CJEU (or where established case law clearly resolves the question), the obligation to refer does not apply. The answer has already been 'clarified' (éclairé) by the CJEU. The court of last instance may apply the existing ruling. However, it must genuinely be the same question — the national court cannot assume it is the same if there are meaningful differences in context.

questions.question: The second CILFIT exception — 'acte clair' — permits a court of last instance not to refer where:

options.A: The court finds the EU law question too complex to formulate as a reference
options.B: The correct application of EU law is so obvious as to leave no scope for any reasonable doubt — having regard to the characteristics of EU law and the particular difficulties of its interpretation
options.C: The EU law question has been answered identically by the majority of member state supreme courts
options.D: The Commission has confirmed in writing that the national court's proposed answer is correct

questions.explanation: Acte clair: the national court of last instance may resolve a point of EU law itself (without referring) where the answer is 'so obvious as to leave no scope for any reasonable doubt'. The CJEU in CILFIT, however, set demanding conditions: the court must be convinced the matter is equally obvious to courts in other member states and to the CJEU itself; it must consider the multilingual nature of EU law; it must be aware that EU law concepts have their own meaning; and it must consider the broader context. The acte clair doctrine was thus hedged with significant qualifications.

questions.question: The third CILFIT exception allows a court of last instance not to refer where the question:

options.A: Is irrelevant to the outcome of the national proceedings
options.B: Is irrelevant to the outcome of the case — the EU law question is not necessary to decide the dispute
options.C: Has been settled by legislation adopted after the reference was considered
options.D: Is purely hypothetical or concerns future legislation not yet in force

questions.explanation: The third CILFIT exception is that where the EU law question is irrelevant to the resolution of the main proceedings — i.e., the answer would make no difference to the outcome — no reference is required. This is the 'necessity' condition: Article 267 requires a reference only where it is 'necessary to enable [the national court] to give judgment'. If the EU law question does not need to be answered to decide the case, no obligation to refer arises. This exception is largely uncontroversial and distinct from the controversial acte clair doctrine.

questions.question: The CJEU in CILFIT warned national courts applying the acte clair doctrine to bear in mind which specific characteristics of EU law?

options.A: That EU law is always more recent than national law and therefore superior
options.B: That EU law is drafted in multiple authentic language versions (all equally binding) and that concepts used in EU law may have a special meaning different from their meaning in national law
options.C: That EU law must always be interpreted in favour of integration
options.D: That EU law applies uniformly and thus national law context is irrelevant to interpretation

questions.explanation: CILFIT identified two key features that make 'obvious clarity' difficult to establish: (1) EU law is drafted in multiple equally authentic languages — a term that appears clear in one language may be ambiguous in another; the national court should check across language versions; (2) EU legal concepts have their own meaning within EU law — terms that appear in national law do not necessarily have the same meaning in EU law. These features make acte clair genuinely rare and the CILFIT conditions demanding.

questions.question: The CJEU revisited and refined the CILFIT doctrine in Consorzio Italian Management (Joined Cases C-561/19) in 2021. The key refinement was:

options.A: The CJEU abolished the CILFIT doctrine and mandated that courts of last instance always refer
options.B: The CJEU restated that the obligation to refer applies unless the question is irrelevant, acte éclairé, or acte clair, but clarified that courts of last instance have greater flexibility in applying acte clair — they need not address every conceivable argument but must be genuinely satisfied no reasonable doubt exists
options.C: The CJEU held that courts of last instance may apply the CILFIT exceptions without giving reasons
options.D: The CJEU extended the CILFIT exceptions to all national courts, not only courts of last instance

questions.explanation: In Consorzio Italian Management (C-561/19, 2021), the CJEU revisited CILFIT in the context of a challenge to an Italian court's failure to refer. The Grand Chamber restated the CILFIT framework but clarified: courts of last instance applying acte clair must genuinely be satisfied (having carried out a diligent examination) that no reasonable doubt exists — but they need not exhaustively address every possible interpretation. The ruling reinforced the importance of the obligation while acknowledging its practical application requires judgment, not mechanical exhaustion of all possibilities.

questions.question: A breach by a court of last instance of the obligation to refer under Article 267(3) TFEU may give rise to which consequences?

options.A: The national court's judgment is automatically void
options.B: The Commission may bring infringement proceedings against the member state under Article 258 TFEU; the individual may bring a state liability (Köbler) claim for judicial breach
options.C: The CJEU may directly reverse the national court's judgment on the EU law point
options.D: The national judge may be personally fined by the Commission

questions.explanation: Two consequences may follow a failure to refer in breach of Article 267(3): (1) Infringement proceedings — the Commission may bring Article 258 proceedings against the member state for its court's failure to refer (though this is extremely rare in practice); (2) Köbler-type state liability — if the failure to refer led to a manifest infringement of EU law causing loss to an individual, a Köbler state liability claim may arise. The national judgment itself is not automatically void — but the failure may be actionable through these routes.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'court of last instance' within the meaning of Article 267(3) TFEU?

options.A: Always the supreme court of the member state — only the very highest court has an obligation to refer
options.B: The court against whose decision there is no judicial remedy under national law in the particular case — this may vary depending on whether leave to appeal has been refused
options.C: Any court above first instance level
options.D: Only courts designated by the member state as courts of last instance for EU law purposes

questions.explanation: The CJEU has adopted a functional/case-by-case definition: a court of last instance is the court against whose decision there is no judicial remedy available in the particular case. This means that even an intermediate appellate court can be a court of last instance if, in the specific case, no appeal is available (e.g., leave to appeal was refused). This functional approach (from Costa v ENEL and subsequent cases) means the obligation to refer is determined by what remedies are actually available in the case, not by the court's general position in the judicial hierarchy.

questions.question: What is the rationale for imposing a stronger obligation on courts of last instance (as opposed to lower courts) to refer EU law questions?

options.A: Courts of last instance have better legal expertise than lower courts
options.B: A court of last instance is the final safeguard against erroneous EU law in national proceedings — if it fails to refer and gives a wrong answer, no further national correction is possible; the only recourse is state liability, which is an imperfect remedy
options.C: Lower courts are closer to the parties and better placed to assess the facts
options.D: Courts of last instance have more resources to handle cross-border EU law questions

questions.explanation: The stronger obligation on courts of last instance reflects their constitutional role: they are the final domestic arbiters. If a lower court gets EU law wrong, an appeal court can correct it. If a court of last instance gets it wrong, there is no further internal correction — the erroneous ruling becomes final. This creates the risk of divergent national interpretations of EU law becoming permanently embedded in national legal systems, undermining the uniform application that Article 267 is designed to ensure.

questions.question: A national court of last instance applies what it believes to be acte clair and decides an EU law point without referring. Its interpretation turns out to be inconsistent with a later CJEU ruling in a different case. What are the legal consequences for the parties to the earlier case?

options.A: The earlier judgment is automatically reopened
options.B: The judgment remains final and binding as a matter of national law — but an individual who suffered loss as a result may bring a Köbler state liability claim if the court's failure to refer and incorrect interpretation constituted a 'manifest infringement' of EU law
options.C: The parties automatically receive a new trial under EU law
options.D: The Commission retroactively corrects the judgment through an administrative decision

questions.explanation: Judgments that become final under national law are not automatically reopened merely because a later CJEU ruling takes a different view. Legal certainty and finality of proceedings are important values. However, if the court of last instance's error was a 'manifest infringement' of EU law (Köbler), the individual may bring a state liability claim for damages in national courts. The threshold is high — not every error grounds liability — but where the CJEU's answer was clearly established and the court departed from it without referring, Köbler liability may arise.

questions.question: Is there any mechanism for parties to appeal a national court of last instance's decision not to refer?

options.A: Yes — parties can appeal directly to the CJEU to compel a reference
options.B: No direct appeal to the CJEU is possible — the decision not to refer belongs to the national court; parties may raise the failure as a ground of Köbler liability or (in some member states) as a constitutional rights violation before the national constitutional court
options.C: Yes — the Commission may appeal to the CJEU on behalf of the individual
options.D: Yes — parties may file an Article 267 application directly with the CJEU

questions.explanation: There is no mechanism for parties to compel a reference or appeal the decision not to refer directly to the CJEU. The decision belongs exclusively to the national court. Parties' options are limited: (1) argue for a Köbler state liability claim in national courts if the failure caused loss through a manifest infringement; (2) in some member states (e.g., Germany, Austria), argue that the failure to refer violated the right to a lawfully constituted court under the national constitution; (3) in extreme cases, raise a complaint before the European Court of Human Rights under Article 6 ECHR (right to fair trial). None of these directly corrects the EU law error.

questions.question: The CJEU in CILFIT also discussed the context in which the question must be assessed to apply acte clair. What is the 'comparative' element of the acte clair analysis?

options.A: The national court must compare its answer with those given by courts in at least three other member states
options.B: The national court must be convinced that the answer would be equally obvious to courts of other member states and to the CJEU — it is not sufficient that the answer seems clear from the perspective of national legal culture alone
options.C: The national court must compare the EU law provision with the corresponding national law provision to check they say the same thing
options.D: The national court must compare the pending case with all previous CJEU cases on the same topic

questions.explanation: The comparative element of CILFIT's acte clair analysis requires the national court to adopt a EU law perspective, not just a national one. The court must be satisfied that the answer would be equally clear to courts of all other member states and to the CJEU itself. This requirement is designed to prevent national courts from applying a 'clear in my legal tradition' test — EU law terms may seem clear in one national context but uncertain in another. It reinforces the demanding nature of the acte clair exception.

questions.question: Can the CJEU itself raise a EU law question for preliminary reference purposes, even if not raised by the parties?

options.A: Yes — the CJEU may raise EU law questions of its own motion in any proceeding
options.B: No — in the context of Article 267, the initiative lies exclusively with the referring national court; the CJEU does not itself generate or trigger references; national courts may raise EU law questions not raised by the parties, however
options.C: Yes — but only in cases involving fundamental rights
options.D: Yes — but only where the Commission has written to the referring court asking it to consider specific EU law issues

questions.explanation: The preliminary reference procedure is initiated by national courts, not by the CJEU. The CJEU cannot compel national courts to refer (except indirectly through the obligation in Article 267(3)) and cannot itself generate references. However, national courts may raise EU law questions of their own motion — they need not wait for parties to raise EU law issues. The duty of conforming interpretation and the principle of sincere cooperation require national courts to apply EU law correctly even if the parties have not invoked it.
